schools.summary: Gill: their introduction into the land is ascribed not to themselves or their leaders but to the Lord as their covenant God.
Henry: the strict caution that follows against communion with the heathen.

themes: the LORD thy God shall bring thee into the land... seven nations greater and mightier than thou
the conquest God's work, not their strength
a people brought in by God to be kept his

schools.summary: Gill: God would deliver them into Israel's hands.
Keil & Delitzsch: smite and utterly destroy them, make no covenant, show no mercy.

themes: thou shalt... utterly destroy them; thou shalt make no covenant with them, nor shew mercy
God's judgment on a corrupt people
a fence about his own against contagion

schools.summary: Gill: unlawful unless they became proselytes (Rahab, married by Salmon, and Ruth the Moabitess); the law's design was to prevent being drawn aside into idolatry, bad consequences having followed such matches.

themes: Neither shalt thou make marriages with them
the bar against idolatry, not race
marry only in the Lord (1 Cor. 7:39)

schools.summary: Gill: they would turn the son away from the pure worship of God, his word, statutes, and ordinances.
Wesley: the manifest danger of apostasy and idolatry; the reason limits the law to the unconverted (else Salmon married Rahab, Matt. 1:5).

themes: they will turn away thy son from following me, that they may serve other gods
the peril of an ungodly union
the law aimed at idolatry, not the converted (Matt. 1:5)

schools.summary: Gill: this was how Israel was to deal with the inhabitants of Canaan.
Ellicott: destroying the objects of their worship would force the inhabitants either to defend their gods, ending in extermination, or to abandon them.

themes: ye shall destroy their altars, and break down their images, and cut down their groves, and burn their graven images
a thorough rooting-out of idolatry
the people of God making no truce with it

schools.summary: Gill: they were holy in the sense of being separated from all other people to the pure worship and service of God, chosen to be a special people unto himself, and therefore were to avoid all idolatry.

themes: thou art an holy people... the LORD thy God hath chosen thee to be a special people unto himself
election the ground of separation
the church a holy nation, a peculiar people (1 Pet. 2:9)

schools.summary: Gill: God loved them and chose them, the one the effect and evidence of the other, but neither because of their number.
Keil & Delitzsch: they were the fewest of all peoples.

themes: The LORD did not set his love upon you, nor choose you, because ye were more in number... for ye were the fewest
election not for any merit in the chosen
sovereign grace, that no flesh glory (1 Cor. 1:27)

schools.summary: Gill: God loved them with an unmerited love — he loved them because he would love them, not for any goodness in them.
Clarke: it was no good in them that induced God to choose them.

themes: because the LORD loved you, and because he would keep the oath... hath the LORD... redeemed you
a free, unmerited, self-moved love
the spring of redemption God's own heart (Rom. 9:16)

schools.summary: Gill: he is the only true and living God, not the idols of the Gentiles, false and lifeless. Calvin: 'thou shalt know' may be read in the future, an experimental knowledge gained by proof.

themes: the faithful God, which keepeth covenant and mercy... to a thousand generations
the constancy of his grace through all ages
his faithfulness known by experience (2 Tim. 2:13)

schools.summary: Gill: he repays them that hate him to their face, openly and at once, not slack; Onkelos and the Targums read it 'in their lifetime'; he will repay the audacious sinner to his face.

themes: repayeth them that hate him to their face, to destroy them
God's justice matching his mercy
his judgment as sure as his grace (Nah. 1:3)

schools.summary: Gill: the laws — moral, ceremonial, and judicial — were to be kept, urged by both promises and threatenings, in hope of reward and fear of punishment, and done, not merely heard.

themes: Thou shalt therefore keep the commandments, and the statutes, and the judgments... to do them
grace the spring of obedience
the chosen proving their calling in doing his word

schools.summary: Gill: they were attentively to listen to the judgments and be careful to observe them.
Keil & Delitzsch: the LORD would keep the covenant and mercy he sware unto the fathers.

themes: if ye hearken to these judgments, and keep, and do them, that the LORD thy God shall keep... the covenant
obedience the way of enjoying the covenant
grace experienced in the path of his ways

schools.summary: Gill: God will love them, as he has done, and rest in his love, giving further proofs of it.
Keil & Delitzsch: the blessing of the fruit of the womb, the land, the corn, wine, oil, and flocks.

themes: he will love thee, and bless thee, and multiply thee: he will also bless the fruit of thy womb, and... thy land
the lavishness of God's covenant goodness
his love a fountain of all good gifts (Eph. 1:3)

schools.summary: Gill: blessed above all people even with temporal blessings, besides the religious — the oracles, covenants, law, and promises.
Keil & Delitzsch: the promise of no barrenness among them or their cattle.

themes: Thou shalt be blessed above all people: there shall not be male or female barren
God's favour on every part of life
the covenant blessing reaching household and flock

schools.summary: Gill: God would take away bodily sickness, prevent it coming, or remove it when come.
Ellicott: the word for 'diseases' is found only in Deuteronomy, the law being in many details a sanitary as well as a moral code.

themes: the LORD will take away from thee all sickness... but will lay them upon all them that hate thee
God's care for the whole man
the LORD the keeper of his people's health (Exod. 15:26)

schools.summary: Gill: they were not to spare but utterly destroy the inhabitants God delivered to them.
Ellicott: 'consume' is literally 'eat up' all the people.

themes: thou shalt consume all the people... neither shalt thou serve their gods; for that will be a snare unto thee
the severity a mercy to their faith
idolatry a snare to the heart (Ps. 106:36)

schools.summary: Gill: these might be secret thoughts arising in the heart, misgivings, fears, and doubts inwardly retained. Calvin: it was a matter of great difficulty to destroy so great a multitude, and despair might drive them to madness.

themes: If thou shalt say in thine heart, These nations are more than I; how can I dispossess them?
the inward misgiving of unbelief
God forestalling the heart's fear with his word

schools.summary: Gill: they were not to be afraid on account of the nations' number or strength.
Keil & Delitzsch: well remember what the LORD did unto Pharaoh and all Egypt.

themes: Thou shalt not be afraid of them: but shalt well remember what the LORD thy God did unto Pharaoh
remembrance the cure for fear
faith fed by memory (1 Sam. 17:37)

schools.summary: Gill: the miracles wrought in Egypt.
Ellicott: the 'temptations' were the repeated summons to Pharaoh, enforced by plagues — trials of his character.

themes: The great temptations which thine eyes saw... so shall the LORD thy God do unto all the people of whom thou art afraid
the past wonders a pledge of victory
the stretched-out arm still for his people

schools.summary: Gill: the hornet, a bold and venomous insect, twice as large as a wasp; Aben Ezra reads it of the leprosy.
Ellicott: takes it literally.

themes: the LORD thy God will send the hornet among them, until they that are left, and hide themselves... be destroyed
the least creature God's instrument
no enemy escaping him (Exod. 23:28)

schools.summary: Gill: they were not to be affrighted at the nations' numbers or gigantic stature.
Keil & Delitzsch: Israel had no need to fear, as Jehovah was in the midst of it, a mighty God and terrible, who would drive out the nations.

themes: Thou shalt not be affrighted at them: for the LORD thy God is among you, a mighty God and terrible
God's presence the cure for terror
greater is he that is in you (1 John 4:4)

schools.summary: Gill: observed for their encouragement, lest seeing all not destroyed at once they fear the work would never be accomplished.
Keil & Delitzsch: the gradual driving-out, lest the beasts of the field increase.

themes: the LORD thy God will put out those nations before thee by little and little
the gradual conquest an act of wisdom and care
God's deliverances coming by degrees, fitted to their strength

schools.summary: Gill: God would deliver them gradually, by little and little, until at length they should all come into Israel's hands and be destroyed with a mighty destruction.

themes: the LORD thy God shall deliver them unto thee, and shall destroy them with a mighty destruction
the gradual working reaching a total end
the certainty of God's purpose

schools.summary: Gill: the kings were very numerous — though seven nations, there were one and thirty kings.
Ellicott: the summary of Joshua's conquest reckons the kings for the cities.

themes: he shall deliver their kings into thine hand... there shall no man be able to stand before thee
God's irresistible help
no power withstanding his people (Josh. 1:5)

schools.summary: Gill: repeated that it might be more strictly performed; unless done, they could not expect the destruction of their enemies.
Keil & Delitzsch: the burning of the graven images.

themes: thou shalt not desire the silver or gold that is on them... lest thou be snared therein
covetousness a subtler snare than the idol
the spoils of sin to be shunned (Josh. 7:21)

schools.summary: Gill: the abomination is an idol, with anything appertaining to its service; this care was taken to prevent idolatry and all appearance of it; the man bringing it would become a cursed thing like it.

themes: Neither shalt thou bring an abomination into thine house, lest thou be a cursed thing like it
an utter abhorrence of idolatry
separating wholly from the unclean thing (2 Cor. 6:17)
